questions.prompt: Why is a daily summary email more valuable than real-time monitoring for most retail bots?

choices.text: Daily summaries surface slow drift that real-time charts miss — gradual slippage degradation, week-over-week PnL trends, anomalies in trade distribution, sector concentration creeping above limits. Real-time monitoring is good for crisis response (kill switch alerts, broker outages) but bad for catching slow problems where you'd habituate to changes. Reading a daily one-pager for 30 seconds catches issues that compound: 'fill latency creeping from 80ms to 200ms' — invisible day-to-day, obvious in 5 daily summaries. Layer both: real-time for crisis (page-worthy alerts only), daily digest for drift (sit-and-read), monthly review for strategic calibration. The 30 seconds/day cost is the cheapest insurance against unnoticed slow failures.

questions.explanation: Daily summaries catch slow drift (slippage creep, trend changes) that real-time dashboards miss.

questions.prompt: Solo trader's alert system goes off at 2 AM with a critical alert. They ignore it and check at 9 AM. Is this OK?

choices.text: Depends — if the alert was a false positive (crypto bot detecting a non-event), it's fine but the alert should be tuned. If it was real (broker outage warning, account anomaly, position move overnight), 7 hours of unanswered alert means whatever was breaking continued to break. Two design responses: (1) tier alerts — page-worthy only for things truly requiring immediate response (kill switch, account compromise), push-only for warnings that can wait until morning, (2) constrain alert hours — if you trade only US regular hours, suppress non-critical alerts outside market hours. The wrong answer is 'alerts I don't answer' — they're noise that masks real signals. Either tune them or commit to answering them.

questions.explanation: Tier alerts by severity and tune hours. Unanswered alerts are noise; tune or commit to respond.
